author: jayhendren
text: certbot-auto can't find libpython2.7 on rhel 6 while running bootstrap/install

author: jayhendren
text:
## My operating system is (include version):

RHEL 6.10

## I installed Certbot with (certbot-auto, OS package manager, pip, etc):

certbot-auto, following the instructions here:

<https://certbot.eff.org/lets-encrypt/centosrhel6-apache>

## I ran this command and it produced this output:

```
[lonetree ~]% sudo certbot-auto --install-only      
/usr/local/bin/certbot-auto: line 282: [: error: integer expression expected

/usr/local/bin/certbot-auto: line 1626: [: error: integer expression expected

```

## Certbot's behavior differed from what I expected because:

When checking with `ldd`, python appears to be linked properly:

```
[lonetree ~]% which python2.7
/opt/rh/python27/root/usr/bin/python2.7
[lonetree ~]% ldd $(which python2.7)
	linux-vdso.so.1 =>  (0x00007ffe7bec1000)
	libpython2.7.so.1.0 => /opt/rh/python27/root/usr/lib64/libpython2.7.so.1.0 (0x00007f7628e5c000)
	libpthread.so.0 => /lib64/libpthread.so.0 (0x00000039d9a00000)
	libdl.so.2 => /lib64/libdl.so.2 (0x00000039d9e00000)
	libutil.so.1 => /lib64/libutil.so.1 (0x00000039df200000)
	libm.so.6 => /lib64/libm.so.6 (0x00000039da600000)
	libc.so.6 => /lib64/libc.so.6 (0x00000039d9600000)
	/lib64/ld-linux-x86-64.so.2 (0x00005580ece6f000)
```

and libpython2.7 exists and is in the linker path:

```
[lonetree ~]% file /opt/rh/python27/root/usr/lib64/libpython2.7.so.1.0
/opt/rh/python27/root/usr/lib64/libpython2.7.so.1.0: ELF 64-bit LSB shared object, x86-64, version 1 (SYSV), dynamically linked, stripped
[lonetree ~]% echo $LD_LIBRARY_PATH
/opt/rh/python27/root/usr/lib64
```

and running python in the same shell session doesn't result in any linker errors:

```
[lonetree ~]% python2.7
Python 2.7.13 (default, Feb  8 2017, 06:30:30) 
[GCC 4.4.7 20120313 (Red Hat 4.4.7-16)] on linux2

>>>
```

author: jayhendren
text:
I got this one figured out. `sudo` was screwing with my environment, even when using `sudo -E`. The main issue seemed to be that `LD_LIBRARY_PATH` was getting wiped out, but even after fixing that, I was still running into environment issues like virtualenv getting really confused and trying to use the wrong Python and stuff like that. Anyway, I ended up writing a wrapper script called `certbot-wrapper` which just sets the right environment. Calling `sudo certbot-wrapper` works like a charm. This is the script:

Leaving this open since I'm not sure whether the certbot team thinks it's worth updating the install documentation based on any of the information here.

author: bmw
text:
Sorry for the trouble [@poohblah](https://github.com/poohblah)! I'm glad you got certbot-auto working with Python 2.7 on RHEL 6.

I don't think I'm going to change any of our documentation though because I don't think Python 2.7 from SCL is normally put into your `PATH` unless you run `source /opt/rh/python27/enable` and when only the OS default of Python 2.6 is found in your `PATH`, certbot-auto walks you through installing Python 3. Because of that, I'm closing this issue, but if you think I misunderstood the problem, feel free to let me know and I'll consider reopening it.

author: bmw
text: [bmw closed this as completed on Apr 4, 2019

author: jayhendren
text: I just think that if you're following the install documentation for your platform, the instructions should "just work" and shouldn't require going on a wild goose chase. Based on my experience, the instructions don't "just work" for RHEL 6 - certbot-auto doesn't work with the default version of Python on RHEL 6 and so a different version needs to be installed, which leads to issues like the ones reported here.

author: bmw
text:
If you can still reproduce the problem, what happens if you don't run `certbot-auto` with `sudo`? certbot-auto calls `sudo` internally when it is needed and I think it may preserve all of the environment variables you need.

If this is the case, one solution here may be just be updating our instructions to tell people to run `certbot-auto` without `sudo`.

author: bmw

text: Spun up a fresh RHEL 6 VM for testing; running without sudo works just fine:

author: bmw
text:
Is installing Python 3.4 the behavior you expected though?

EDIT: Was Python 2.7 available in your PATH on this machine like it presumably was in your initial post?

author: jayhendren
text:
Ah nevermind disregard that post. I didn't fully read your request.

So I played around a bit and basically I'm able to trigger the issue on any system that has a non-default Python installation in `$PATH`. When that's the case, whether or not I use sudo or let certbot-auto internally call sudo, it doesn't seem to make a difference:

So I think the installation instructions seem to be fine for a fresh-out-of-the-box RHEL 6 system, but for something that already has another Python in `$PATH`, it doesn't seem to work so well.

author: bmw
text:
I think the error with conda is slightly different (as far as I can tell, `virtualenv` with Python from Conda is broken), but it's good to know about both issues regardless.

To be honest, we probably won't get to this as RHEL 6 is EOL'd next year and we're trying to replace `certbot-auto` (and have stopped accepting changes to the script outside of regressions), but we'll keep this open just in case.

author: jayhendren
text: Ok, sounds good! Thanks for your help!

author: stale
text: We've made a lot of changes to Certbot since this issue was opened. If you still have this issue with an up-to-date version of Certbot, can you please add a comment letting us know? This helps us to better see what issues are still affecting our users. If there is no activity in the next 30 days, this issue will be automatically closed.
